title: No Dollars to Uyghur Forced Labor Act
short_title: No Dollars to Uyghur Forced Labor Act
plain_summary: This law stops two U.S. government agencies from spending money on programs that use products made in China's Xinjiang region, where Uyghur people face forced labor. The ban includes goods made directly in Xinjiang, products from companies that get materials from Xinjiang, and items from businesses that use forced labor connected to that region. The State Department can sometimes make exceptions to this rule, but only if they tell Congress first and get a written promise from their partners that they will not use these products and will create a system to make sure they follow the rules.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the No Dollars to Uyghur Forced Labor Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Prohibition on Use of Funds Supporting Any Activities Within the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of the People's Republic of China
sections.text: The United States government cannot use money given to the State Department or the United States Agency for International Development in certain ways. These agencies cannot use this money to create, design, plan, announce, put into action, or carry out any policy, program, or contract that knowingly uses goods that were mined, made, or manufactured completely or partly in the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of China. They also cannot use goods made by certain companies that are on a government list. However, the Secretary of State can allow these activities in special cases. To do this, the Secretary must get a written promise from the program partner or contractor. This promise must say they will not use goods from the Xinjiang region and will create a system to make sure they follow these rules. The Secretary must also tell the leaders of certain committees in Congress at least fifteen days before allowing the activity. The activity must also not be banned by other laws. Every year for three years, the Secretary of State must write a report for Congress. This report must describe any activities that broke these rules without permission, any problems with enforcing these rules, and a plan to better enforce them in the future. In this law, covered entity means a company that appears on a special government list created by an earlier law called Public Law one hundred seventeen dash seventy eight. Forced labor has the same meaning as defined in section three hundred seven of the Tariff Act of nineteen thirty.
